title: German literature

infobox.value: ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Deutschsprachige Literatur*) comprises those [literary](/wiki/Literature "Literature") texts written in the [German language](/wiki/German_language "German language"). This includes literature written in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), the German parts of [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") and [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein "Liechtenstein"), [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg"), [South Tyrol](/wiki/South_Tyrol "South Tyrol") in Italy and to a lesser extent works of the [German diaspora](/wiki/German_diaspora "German diaspora"). German literature of the modern period is mostly in [Standard German](/wiki/Standard_German "Standard German"), but there are some currents of literature influenced to a greater or lesser degree by [dialects](/wiki/German_dialects "German dialects") (e.g. [Alemannic](/wiki/Alemannic_literature "Alemannic literature")).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Printing3_Walk_of_Ideas_Berlin.JPG/250px-Printing3_Walk_of_Ideas_Berlin.JPG)](/wiki/File:Printing3_Walk_of_Ideas_Berlin.JPG)

Sculpture in [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") depicting the names of [Grass](/wiki/G%C3%BCnter_Grass "Günter Grass"), [Arendt](/wiki/Hannah_Arendt "Hannah Arendt"), [Heine](/wiki/Heinrich_Heine "Heinrich Heine"), [Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther"), [Kant](/wiki/Immanuel_Kant "Immanuel Kant"), [Seghers](/wiki/Anna_Seghers "Anna Seghers"), [Hegel](/wiki/Georg_Wilhelm_Friedrich_Hegel "Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel"), [Brothers Grimm](/wiki/Brothers_Grimm "Brothers Grimm"), [Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx"), [Böll](/wiki/Heinrich_B%C3%B6ll "Heinrich Böll"), [Schiller](/wiki/Friedrich_Schiller "Friedrich Schiller"), [Lessing](/wiki/Gotthold_Ephraim_Lessing "Gotthold Ephraim Lessing"), [Hesse](/wiki/Hermann_Hesse "Hermann Hesse"), [Fontane](/wiki/Theodor_Fontane "Theodor Fontane"), [Mann](/wiki/Thomas_Mann "Thomas Mann"), [Brecht](/wiki/Berthold_Brecht "Berthold Brecht") and [Goethe](/wiki/Johann_Wolfgang_von_Goethe "Johann Wolfgang von Goethe").

**German literature** ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Deutschsprachige Literatur*) comprises those [literary](/wiki/Literature "Literature") texts written in the [German language](/wiki/German_language "German language"). This includes literature written in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), the German parts of [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") and [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein "Liechtenstein"), [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg"), [South Tyrol](/wiki/South_Tyrol "South Tyrol") in Italy and to a lesser extent works of the [German diaspora](/wiki/German_diaspora "German diaspora"). German literature of the modern period is mostly in [Standard German](/wiki/Standard_German "Standard German"), but there are some currents of literature influenced to a greater or lesser degree by [dialects](/wiki/German_dialects "German dialects") (e.g. [Alemannic](/wiki/Alemannic_literature "Alemannic literature")).

Medieval German literature is [literature](/wiki/Literature "Literature") written in Germany, stretching from the [Carolingian dynasty](/wiki/Carolingian_dynasty "Carolingian dynasty"); various dates have been given for the end of the German literary Middle Ages, the [Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") (1517) being the last possible cut-off point. The Old High German period is reckoned to run until about the mid-11th century; the most famous works are the *[Hildebrandslied](/wiki/Hildebrandslied "Hildebrandslied")* and a heroic epic known as the *[Heliand](/wiki/Heliand "Heliand")*. [Middle High German](/wiki/Middle_High_German "Middle High German") starts in the 12th century; the key works include *[The Ring](/wiki/The_Ring_(poem) "The Ring (poem)")* (c. 1410) and the poems of [Oswald von Wolkenstein](/wiki/Oswald_von_Wolkenstein "Oswald von Wolkenstein") and [Johannes von Tepl](/wiki/Johannes_von_Tepl "Johannes von Tepl"). The Baroque period (1600 to 1720) was one of the most fertile times in German literature. Modern literature in German begins with the authors of [the Enlightenment](/wiki/The_Enlightenment "The Enlightenment") (such as [Herder](/wiki/Johann_Gottfried_Herder "Johann Gottfried Herder")). The Sensibility movement of the 1750s–1770s ended with [Goethe](/wiki/Goethe "Goethe")'s best-selling *[The Sorrows of Young Werther](/wiki/The_Sorrows_of_Young_Werther "The Sorrows of Young Werther")* (1774). The [Sturm und Drang](/wiki/Sturm_und_Drang "Sturm und Drang") and [Weimar Classicism](/wiki/Weimar_Classicism "Weimar Classicism") movements were led by [Johann Wolfgang von Goethe](/wiki/Johann_Wolfgang_von_Goethe "Johann Wolfgang von Goethe") and [Friedrich Schiller](/wiki/Friedrich_Schiller "Friedrich Schiller"). [German Romanticism](/wiki/German_Romanticism "German Romanticism") was the dominant movement of the late 18th and early 19th centuries.

[Biedermeier](/wiki/Biedermeier "Biedermeier") refers to the literature, music, the visual arts and interior design in the period between the years 1815 ([Vienna Congress](/wiki/Vienna_Congress "Vienna Congress")), the end of the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), and 1848, the year of the [European revolutions](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1848 "Revolutions of 1848"). Under the [Nazi regime](/wiki/Nazi "Nazi"), some authors went into exile (*Exilliteratur*) and others submitted to censorship ("internal emigration", *Innere Emigration*). The [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature") has been awarded to German language authors fourteen times (as of 2023), or the third most often, behind only French language authors (with 16 laureates) and English language authors (with 32 laureates) with winners including [Thomas Mann](/wiki/Thomas_Mann "Thomas Mann"), [Hermann Hesse](/wiki/Hermann_Hesse "Hermann Hesse"), [Günter Grass](/wiki/G%C3%BCnter_Grass "Günter Grass"), and [Peter Handke](/wiki/Peter_Handke "Peter Handke").

## Periodization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_literature&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Periodization")]

[Periodization](/wiki/Periodization "Periodization") is not an [exact science](/wiki/Exact_science "Exact science") but the following list contains movements or time periods typically used in discussing German literature. It seems worth noting that the periods of [medieval](/wiki/Medieval "Medieval") German literature span two or three centuries, those of early modern German literature span one century, and those of modern German literature each span one or two decades. The closer one nears the present, the more debated the periodizations become.

## Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_literature&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

Medieval German literature refers to [literature](/wiki/Literature "Literature") written in Germany, stretching from the [Carolingian dynasty](/wiki/Carolingian_dynasty "Carolingian dynasty"); various dates have been given for the end of the German literary Middle Ages, the [Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") (1517) being the last possible cut-off point.

### Old High German

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_literature&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Old High German")]

Main article: [Old High German literature](/wiki/Old_High_German_literature "Old High German literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Hildebrandslied1.jpg/250px-Hildebrandslied1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hildebrandslied1.jpg)

The first page of the [Hildebrandslied](/wiki/Hildebrandslied "Hildebrandslied") manuscript

The Old High German period is reckoned to run until about the mid-11th century, though the boundary to Early Middle High German (second half of the 11th century) is not clear-cut.

The earliest texts date from the second half of the 8th century: translation aids ([glosses](/wiki/Glosses "Glosses") and [glossaries](/wiki/Glossaries "Glossaries")) for those learning to read [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") and translations of Latin Christian texts ([prayers](/wiki/Prayers "Prayers"), [creeds](/wiki/Creeds "Creeds"), [confessions](/wiki/Confession_(religion) "Confession (religion)")) for use in [missionary](/wiki/Missionary "Missionary") or pastoral work among the lay population. Translations and, later, adaptations of Latin Christian texts, continue throughout the period, and are seen in [Otfrid](/wiki/Otfrid_of_Weissenburg "Otfrid of Weissenburg")'s [gospel harmony](/wiki/Gospel_harmony "Gospel harmony") in the 9th century and the extensive works of [Notker III](/wiki/Notker_III "Notker III") in the early 11th century.

The most famous work in OHG is the *[Hildebrandslied](/wiki/Hildebrandslied "Hildebrandslied")*, a short piece of Germanic alliterative heroic verse which besides the *[Muspilli](/wiki/Muspilli "Muspilli")* is the sole survivor of what must have been a vast oral tradition. Another important work, in the northern dialect of Old Saxon, is a life of Christ in the style of a heroic epic known as the *[Heliand](/wiki/Heliand "Heliand")*.

### Middle High German

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_literature&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Middle High German")]

Main article: [Middle High German literature](/wiki/Middle_High_German_literature "Middle High German literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Codex_Manesse_Walther_von_der_Vogelweide.jpg/250px-Codex_Manesse_Walther_von_der_Vogelweide.jpg)](/wiki/File:Codex_Manesse_Walther_von_der_Vogelweide.jpg)

Portrait of [Walther von der Vogelweide](/wiki/Walther_von_der_Vogelweide "Walther von der Vogelweide") from the [Codex Manesse](/wiki/Codex_Manesse "Codex Manesse") (Folio 124r)

[Middle High German](/wiki/Middle_High_German "Middle High German") proper runs from the beginning of the 12th century, and in the second half of the 12th century, there was a sudden intensification of activity, leading to a 60-year "golden age" of medieval German literature referred to as the *mittelhochdeutsche Blütezeit* (1170–1230). This was the period of the blossoming of MHG lyric poetry, particularly *[Minnesang](/wiki/Minnesang "Minnesang")* (the German variety of the originally French tradition of [courtly love](/wiki/Courtly_love "Courtly love")). One of the most important of these poets was [Walther von der Vogelweide](/wiki/Walther_von_der_Vogelweide "Walther von der Vogelweide"). The same sixty years saw the composition of the most important courtly romances. These are written in rhyming couplets, and again draw on French models such as [Chrétien de Troyes](/wiki/Chr%C3%A9tien_de_Troyes "Chrétien de Troyes"), many of them relating [Arthurian](/wiki/Arthurian "Arthurian") material, for example, *[Parzival](/wiki/Parzival "Parzival")* by [Wolfram von Eschenbach](/wiki/Wolfram_von_Eschenbach "Wolfram von Eschenbach"). The third literary movement of these years was a new revamping of the heroic tradition, in which the ancient Germanic oral tradition can still be discerned, but tamed and Christianized and adapted for the court. These high medieval [heroic epics](/wiki/Heroic_epic "Heroic epic") are written in rhymed strophes, not the alliterative verse of Germanic prehistory (for example, the *[Nibelungenlied](/wiki/Nibelungenlied "Nibelungenlied")*).

The Middle High German period is conventionally taken to end in 1350, while the [Early New High German](/wiki/Early_New_High_German "Early New High German") is taken to begin with the [German Renaissance](/wiki/German_Renaissance "German Renaissance"), after the invention of movable type in the mid-15th century. Therefore, the literature of the late 14th and the early 15th century falls, as it were, in the cracks between Middle and New High German, and can be classified as either. Works of this transitional period include *[The Ring](/wiki/The_Ring_(poem) "The Ring (poem)")* (c. 1410), the poems of [Oswald von Wolkenstein](/wiki/Oswald_von_Wolkenstein "Oswald von Wolkenstein") and [Johannes von Tepl](/wiki/Johannes_von_Tepl "Johannes von Tepl"), the German versions of *[Pontus and Sidonia](/wiki/Pontus_and_Sidonia "Pontus and Sidonia")*, and arguably the works of [Hans Folz](/wiki/Hans_Folz "Hans Folz") and [Sebastian Brant](/wiki/Sebastian_Brant "Sebastian Brant") (*[Ship of Fools](/wiki/Ship_of_Fools_(satire) "Ship of Fools (satire)")*, 1494), among others. The *Volksbuch* ([chapbook](/wiki/Chapbook "Chapbook")) tradition which would flourish in the 16th century also finds its origin in the second half of the 15th century.

### Baroque period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_literature&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Baroque period")]

The Baroque period (1600 to 1720) was one of the most fertile times in German literature. Many writers reflected the horrible experiences of the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War"), in [poetry](/wiki/Poetry "Poetry") and [prose](/wiki/Prose "Prose"). [Grimmelshausen](/wiki/Hans_Jakob_Christoffel_von_Grimmelshausen "Hans Jakob Christoffel von Grimmelshausen")'s adventures of the young and naïve Simplicissimus, in the eponymous book *[Simplicius Simplicissimus](/wiki/Simplicius_Simplicissimus "Simplicius Simplicissimus")*,[[2]](#cite_note-2) became the most famous novel of the Baroque period. [Martin Opitz](/wiki/Martin_Opitz "Martin Opitz") established rules for the "purity" of language, style, verse and rhyme. [Andreas Gryphius](/wiki/Andreas_Gryphius "Andreas Gryphius") and [Daniel Caspar von Lohenstein](/wiki/Daniel_Caspar_von_Lohenstein "Daniel Caspar von Lohenstein") wrote German language [tragedies](/wiki/Tragedy "Tragedy"), or *Trauerspiele*, often on Classical themes and frequently quite violent. Erotic, religious and [occasional poetry](/wiki/Occasional_poetry "Occasional poetry") appeared in both German and Latin. [Sibylle Ursula von Braunschweig-Lüneburg](/wiki/Sibylle_Ursula_von_Braunschweig-L%C3%BCneburg "Sibylle Ursula von Braunschweig-Lüneburg") wrote part of a novel, *Die Durchlauchtige Syrerin Aramena* (*Aramena, the noble Syrian lady*), which when complete would be the most famous courtly novel in German Baroque literature; it was finished by her brother [Anton Ulrich](/wiki/Anthony_Ulrich,_Duke_of_Brunswick-Wolfenb%C3%BCttel "Anthony Ulrich, Duke of Brunswick-Wolfenbüttel") and edited by [Sigmund von Birken](/wiki/Sigmund_von_Birken "Sigmund von Birken").[[3]](#cite_note-Brown2012-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Catling2000-4)

### Sensibility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_literature&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Sensibility")]

*Empfindsamkeit* / Sensibility (1750s–1770s)
[Friedrich Gottlieb Klopstock](/wiki/Friedrich_Gottlieb_Klopstock "Friedrich Gottlieb Klopstock") (1724–1803), [Christian Fürchtegott Gellert](/wiki/Christian_F%C3%BCrchtegott_Gellert "Christian Fürchtegott Gellert") (1715–1769), [Sophie de La Roche](/wiki/Sophie_de_La_Roche "Sophie de La Roche") (1730–1807). The period culminates and ends in [Goethe](/wiki/Goethe "Goethe")'s best-selling *[Die Leiden des jungen Werthers](/wiki/Die_Leiden_des_jungen_Werthers "Die Leiden des jungen Werthers")* (1774).

### Sturm und Drang

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_literature&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Sturm und Drang")]

Main article: [Sturm und Drang](/wiki/Sturm_und_Drang "Sturm und Drang")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Goethe.png/250px-Goethe.png)](/wiki/File:Goethe.png)

[Johann Wolfgang von Goethe](/wiki/Johann_Wolfgang_von_Goethe "Johann Wolfgang von Goethe") c. 1775

*Sturm und Drang* (the conventional translation is "Storm and Stress"; a more literal translation, however, might be *storm and urge*, *storm and longing*, or *storm and impulse*) is the name of a movement in German literature and [music](/wiki/Music "Music") taking place from the late 1760s through the early 1780s in which individual [subjectivity](/wiki/Subjectivity "Subjectivity") and, in particular, extremes of emotion were given free expression in response to the confines of rationalism imposed by [the Enlightenment](/wiki/The_Enlightenment "The Enlightenment") and associated [aesthetic](/wiki/Aesthetic "Aesthetic") movements. The philosopher [Johann Georg Hamann](/wiki/Johann_Georg_Hamann "Johann Georg Hamann") is considered to be the ideologue of *Sturm und Drang*, and [Johann Wolfgang von Goethe](/wiki/Johann_Wolfgang_von_Goethe "Johann Wolfgang von Goethe") was a notable proponent of the movement, though he and [Friedrich Schiller](/wiki/Friedrich_Schiller "Friedrich Schiller") ended their period of association with it, initiating what would become [Weimar Classicism](/wiki/Weimar_Classicism "Weimar Classicism").

The first ideas of [Romanticism](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism") arose from this movement, directly criticizing the Enlightenment's position that humans can fully comprehend the world through [rationality](/wiki/Rationality "Rationality") alone, suggesting that [intuition](/wiki/Intuition "Intuition") and [emotion](/wiki/Emotion "Emotion") are key components of [insight](/wiki/Insight "Insight") and understanding.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Published in 1774, *[The Sorrows of Young Werther](/wiki/The_Sorrows_of_Young_Werther "The Sorrows of Young Werther")* by Goethe began to shape the Romantic movement and its ideals.

## 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_literature&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 19th century")]

### German Classicism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_literature&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: German Classicism")]

Main article: [Weimar Classicism](/wiki/Weimar_Classicism "Weimar Classicism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Oer-Weimarer_Musenhof.jpg/250px-Oer-Weimarer_Musenhof.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oer-Weimarer_Musenhof.jpg)

*[Weimar courtyard of the muses](/wiki/Weimar_courtyard_of_the_muses "Weimar courtyard of the muses"). [Schiller](/wiki/Friedrich_Schiller "Friedrich Schiller") reading to the court in [Tiefurt](/wiki/Schloss_Tiefurt "Schloss Tiefurt").* (1860) by [Theobald von Oer](/wiki/Theobald_von_Oer "Theobald von Oer").

Weimar Classicism ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language") “*Weimarer Klassik*” and “*Weimarer Klassizismus*”) is a [cultural](/wiki/Cultural_movement "Cultural movement") and [literary movement](/wiki/Literary_movement "Literary movement") of [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), and its central ideas were originally propounded by [Johann Wolfgang von Goethe](/wiki/Johann_Wolfgang_von_Goethe "Johann Wolfgang von Goethe") and [Johann Christoph Friedrich von Schiller](/wiki/Johann_Christoph_Friedrich_von_Schiller "Johann Christoph Friedrich von Schiller") during the period 1786 to 1805.

The *Weimarer Klassik* movement began in 1771 when [Duchess Anna Amalia of Brunswick-Wolfenbüttel](/wiki/Duchess_Anna_Amalia_of_Brunswick-Wolfenb%C3%BCttel "Duchess Anna Amalia of Brunswick-Wolfenbüttel") invited the [Seyler Theatre Company](/wiki/Seyler_Theatre_Company "Seyler Theatre Company") led by [Abel Seyler](/wiki/Abel_Seyler "Abel Seyler"), pioneers of the *[Sturm und Drang](/wiki/Sturm_und_Drang "Sturm und Drang")* movement, to her court in Weimar. The Seyler company was soon thereafter followed by [Christoph Martin Wieland](/wiki/Christoph_Martin_Wieland "Christoph Martin Wieland"), then Goethe, [Johann Gottfried Herder](/wiki/Johann_Gottfried_Herder "Johann Gottfried Herder") and finally Schiller. The movement was eventually centred upon Goethe and Schiller, previously also exponents of the *[Sturm und Drang](/wiki/Sturm_und_Drang "Sturm und Drang")* movement, during the period of 1786–1805.

### Romanticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_literature&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Romanticism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Heinrich_Heine_1827.jpg/250px-Heinrich_Heine_1827.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heinrich_Heine_1827.jpg)

[Heinrich Heine](/wiki/Heinrich_Heine "Heinrich Heine"), 1827

[German Romanticism](/wiki/German_Romanticism "German Romanticism") was the dominant movement of the late 18th and early 19th centuries. German Romanticism developed relatively late compared to its [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") counterpart, coinciding in its early years with the movement known as [German Classicism](/wiki/German_Classicism "German Classicism") or [Weimar Classicism](/wiki/Weimar_Classicism "Weimar Classicism"), which it opposed. In contrast to the seriousness of English Romanticism, the German variety is notable for valuing humor and wit as well as beauty. The early German romantics tried to create a new synthesis of art, philosophy, and science, looking to the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") as a simpler, more integrated period. As time went on, however, they became increasingly aware of the tenuousness of the unity they were seeking. Later German Romanticism emphasized the tension between the everyday world and the seemingly irrational and supernatural projections of creative genius. [Heinrich Heine](/wiki/Heinrich_Heine "Heinrich Heine") in particular criticized the tendency of the early romantics to look to the medieval past for a model of unity in art and society.

### Biedermeier and Vormärz

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_literature&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Biedermeier and Vormärz")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Chamisso_Adelbert_von_1781-1838.png/120px-Chamisso_Adelbert_von_1781-1838.png)](/wiki/File:Chamisso_Adelbert_von_1781-1838.png)

[Adelbert von Chamisso](/wiki/Adelbert_von_Chamisso "Adelbert von Chamisso")

[Biedermeier](/wiki/Biedermeier "Biedermeier") refers to work in the fields of literature, music, the visual arts and interior design in the period between the years 1815 ([Vienna Congress](/wiki/Vienna_Congress "Vienna Congress")), the end of the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), and 1848, the year of the [European revolutions](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1848 "Revolutions of 1848") and contrasts with the [Romantic](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism") era which preceded it. Typical Biedermeier poets are [Annette von Droste-Hülshoff](/wiki/Annette_von_Droste-H%C3%BClshoff "Annette von Droste-Hülshoff"), [Adelbert von Chamisso](/wiki/Adelbert_von_Chamisso "Adelbert von Chamisso"), [Eduard Mörike](/wiki/Eduard_M%C3%B6rike "Eduard Mörike"), and [Wilhelm Müller](/wiki/Wilhelm_M%C3%BCller "Wilhelm Müller"), the last three named having well-known musical settings by [Robert Schumann](/wiki/Robert_Schumann "Robert Schumann"), [Hugo Wolf](/wiki/Hugo_Wolf "Hugo Wolf") and [Franz Schubert](/wiki/Franz_Schubert "Franz Schubert") respectively.

[Young Germany](/wiki/Young_Germany "Young Germany") (*Junges Deutschland*) was a loose group of [Vormärz](/wiki/Vorm%C3%A4rz "Vormärz") writers which existed from about 1830 to 1850. It was essentially a youth movement (similar to those that had swept [France](/wiki/France "France") and [Ireland](/wiki/Young_Ireland "Young Ireland") and originated in [Italy](/wiki/Young_Italy_(historical) "Young Italy (historical)")). Its main proponents were [Karl Gutzkow](/wiki/Karl_Gutzkow "Karl Gutzkow"), [Heinrich Laube](/wiki/Heinrich_Laube "Heinrich Laube"), [Theodor Mundt](/wiki/Theodor_Mundt "Theodor Mundt") and [Ludolf Wienbarg](/wiki/Ludolf_Wienbarg "Ludolf Wienbarg"); [Heinrich Heine](/wiki/Heinrich_Heine "Heinrich Heine"), [Ludwig Börne](/wiki/Ludwig_B%C3%B6rne "Ludwig Börne") and [Georg Büchner](/wiki/Georg_B%C3%BCchner "Georg Büchner") were also considered part of the movement. The wider circle included [Willibald Alexis](/wiki/Willibald_Alexis "Willibald Alexis"), [Adolf Glassbrenner](/wiki/Adolf_Glassbrenner "Adolf Glassbrenner") and [Gustav Kühne](/w/index.php?title=Gustav_K%C3%BChne&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gustav Kühne (page does not exist)").

### Realism and Naturalism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_literature&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Realism and Naturalism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Fontane1860.jpg/250px-Fontane1860.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fontane1860.jpg)

[Theodor Fontane](/wiki/Theodor_Fontane "Theodor Fontane") (c. 1860).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Gerhart_Hauptmann_nobel.jpg/250px-Gerhart_Hauptmann_nobel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gerhart_Hauptmann_nobel.jpg)

[Gerhart Hauptmann](/wiki/Gerhart_Hauptmann "Gerhart Hauptmann") is counted among the most important promoters of [literary naturalism](/wiki/Naturalism_(literature) "Naturalism (literature)") and received the [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/1912_Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "1912 Nobel Prize in Literature") in 1912.

Poetic [Realism](/wiki/Literary_realism "Literary realism") (1848–1890): 19th-century German Realism in literature, emerging after 1848, focused on depicting everyday life with accuracy and restraint. Writers moved away from Romantic idealism, emphasizing the middle class, social norms, and personal morality. Rather than political upheaval, they portrayed quiet, internal conflicts and the subtle ironies of bourgeois life. This literary realism aimed for poetic realism, blending realistic detail with refined artistic form. Key authors include [Theodor Fontane](/wiki/Theodor_Fontane "Theodor Fontane"), [Gustav Freitag](/wiki/Gustav_Freitag "Gustav Freitag"), [Gottfried Keller](/wiki/Gottfried_Keller "Gottfried Keller"), [Wilhelm Raabe](/wiki/Wilhelm_Raabe "Wilhelm Raabe"), [Adalbert Stifter](/wiki/Adalbert_Stifter "Adalbert Stifter"), [Theodor Storm](/wiki/Theodor_Storm "Theodor Storm"). Their works reflect a belief in gradual societal progress, often with a sense of quiet resignation and irony, avoiding extremes of sentimentality or revolutionary zeal.

[Naturalism](/wiki/Naturalism_(literature) "Naturalism (literature)") (1880–1900): German Naturalism in literature, emerging in the late 19th century, aimed to depict life with scientific objectivity and detailed realism. Influenced by French Naturalists like Émile Zola, it focused on the deterministic effects of environment, heredity, and social conditions on individuals, especially the marginalized. Naturalist writers rejected idealization, portraying poverty, disease, and moral decay with stark precision. Dialogue often mimicked actual speech patterns, including dialects. Prominent figures include Gerhart Hauptmann, whose play Before Sunrise exemplifies the movement. German Naturalism sought to expose societal injustices, emphasizing a mechanistic, cause-and-effect view of human behavior shaped by forces beyond individual control. Key authors include naturalistic writers were [Gerhart Hauptmann](/wiki/Gerhart_Hauptmann "Gerhart Hauptmann"), [Arno Holz](/wiki/Arno_Holz "Arno Holz") and [Johannes Schlaf](/wiki/Johannes_Schlaf "Johannes Schlaf").

### Well known writers of the 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_literature&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Well known writers of the 20th century")]

A well-known writer of [German literature](/wiki/German_Literature "German Literature") was [Franz Kafka](/wiki/Franz_Kafka "Franz Kafka"). A Kafka novel, *[The Trial](/wiki/The_Trial "The Trial")*, was ranked #3 on [Le Monde's 100 Books of the Century](/wiki/Le_Monde%27s_100_Books_of_the_Century "Le Monde's 100 Books of the Century").[[6]](#cite_note-6) Kafka's iconic writing style that captures themes of bureaucracy and existentialism resulted in the coining of the term “Kafkaesque.”[[7]](#cite_note-7) Kafka's writing allowed a peek into his melancholic life, one where he felt isolated from all human beings, one of his inspirations for writing.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Kafka is therefore widely regarded as a major figure of [20th-century literature](/wiki/20th-century_literature "20th-century literature") with his work been interpreted as exploring themes of [alienation](/wiki/Social_alienation "Social alienation"), [existential anxiety](/wiki/Existential_anxiety "Existential anxiety"), [guilt](/wiki/Guilt_(emotion) "Guilt (emotion)"), and [absurdity](/wiki/Absurdity "Absurdity").[[9]](#cite_note-Britannica-9) His best-known works include the novella *[The Metamorphosis](/wiki/The_Metamorphosis "The Metamorphosis")* (1915) and the novels *[The Trial](/wiki/The_Trial "The Trial")* (1924) and *[The Castle](/wiki/The_Castle_(novel) "The Castle (novel)")* (1926).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Grimm.jpg/250px-Grimm.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grimm.jpg)

[Wilhelm Grimm](/wiki/Wilhelm_Grimm "Wilhelm Grimm") (left) and [Jacob Grimm](/wiki/Jacob_Grimm "Jacob Grimm") (right), portrayed by [Elisabeth Jerichau-Baumann](/wiki/Elisabeth_Jerichau-Baumann "Elisabeth Jerichau-Baumann") (1855)

The [Brothers Grimm](/wiki/Brothers_Grimm "Brothers Grimm") ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *die Brüder Grimm* or *die Gebrüder Grimm*), [Jacob](/wiki/Jacob_Grimm "Jacob Grimm") (1785–1863) and [Wilhelm](/wiki/Wilhelm_Grimm "Wilhelm Grimm") (1786–1859), were [German](/wiki/Germans "Germans") academics who together collected and published [folklore](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore"). The brothers are among the best-known storytellers of [folktales](/wiki/Oral_tradition "Oral tradition"), popularizing stories such as "[Cinderella](/wiki/Cinderella "Cinderella")" ("*Aschenputtel*"), "[The Frog Prince](/wiki/The_Frog_Prince_(story) "The Frog Prince (story)")" ("*Der Froschkönig*"), "[Hansel and Gretel](/wiki/Hansel_and_Gretel "Hansel and Gretel")" ("*Hänsel und Gretel*"), "[Town Musicians of Bremen](/wiki/Town_Musicians_of_Bremen "Town Musicians of Bremen")" ("*Die Bremer Stadtmusikanten*"), "[Little Red Riding Hood](/wiki/Little_Red_Riding_Hood "Little Red Riding Hood")" ("*Rotkäppchen*"), "[Rapunzel](/wiki/Rapunzel "Rapunzel")", "[Rumpelstiltskin](/wiki/Rumpelstiltskin "Rumpelstiltskin")" ("*Rumpelstilzchen*"), "[Sleeping Beauty](/wiki/Sleeping_Beauty "Sleeping Beauty")" ("*Dornröschen*"), and "[Snow White](/wiki/Snow_White "Snow White")" ("*Schneewittchen*"). Their first collection of folktales, *[Children's and Household Tales](/wiki/Grimms%27_Fairy_Tales "Grimms' Fairy Tales")* (*Kinder- und Hausmärchen*), was first published in 1812.

A Physician [Heinrich Hoffmann](/wiki/Heinrich_Hoffmann_(author) "Heinrich Hoffmann (author)") (1809–1894) written a book filled with [cautionary tales](/wiki/Cautionary_tale "Cautionary tale") called: [Der Struwwelpeter](/wiki/Struwwelpeter "Struwwelpeter") it was published in 1845 and was given as a Christmas gift for his 3-year-old son. After that, the anniversary of Struwwelpeter and its legacy still goes on until 1864 it was reillustrated by the author himself and it was still claimed as a classic.

Another book was also inspired by Der Struwwelpeter was a book Called: (Max und Moritz) "[Max and Moritz](/wiki/Max_and_Moritz "Max and Moritz")" written and illustrated by a poet and artist [Wilhelm Busch](/wiki/Wilhelm_Busch "Wilhelm Busch") (1832–1908) and it was published in 1865 it certainly became popular because of its goofy cartoon style from Busch's mind and imagination with the word of phrases and rhymes to it.

The rise of [Romanticism](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism") in [19th-century Europe](/wiki/History_of_Europe#From_revolution_to_imperialism_(1789–1914) "History of Europe") revived interest in traditional folk stories, which to the Brothers Grimm represented a pure form of national literature and culture. With the goal of researching a scholarly treatise on folktales, they established a methodology for collecting and recording folk stories that became the basis for [folklore studies](/wiki/Folkloristics "Folkloristics"). Between 1812 and 1857 their first collection was revised and republished many times, growing from 86 stories to more than 200. In addition to writing and modifying folktales, the brothers wrote collections of well-respected [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_mythology "Germanic mythology") and [Scandinavian](/wiki/Norse_mythology "Norse mythology") [mythologies](/wiki/Mythology "Mythology"), and in 1838 they began writing a definitive German dictionary (*[Deutsches Wörterbuch](/wiki/Deutsches_W%C3%B6rterbuch "Deutsches Wörterbuch")*), which they were unable to finish.

The popularity of the Grimms' collected folktales has endured. They are available in more than 100 translations and have been adapted by renowned filmmakers, including [Lotte Reiniger](/wiki/Lotte_Reiniger "Lotte Reiniger") and [Walt Disney](/wiki/Walt_Disney "Walt Disney"), in films such as *[Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs](/wiki/Snow_White_and_the_Seven_Dwarfs_(1937_film) "Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs (1937 film)")*.

## Nobel Prize laureates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_literature&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Nobel Prize laureates")]

Further information: [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature")

The Nobel Prize in Literature has been awarded to German-language authors fourteen times (as of 2020), tying with French-language authors, or the second most often after English-language authors (with 32).

The following writers are from Germany unless stated otherwise:

| [Thomas Mann](/wiki/Thomas_Mann "Thomas Mann") (1875–1955) | [Hermann Hesse](/wiki/Hermann_Hesse "Hermann Hesse") (1877–1962) | [Günter Grass](/wiki/G%C3%BCnter_Grass "Günter Grass") (1927–2015) |
